question.text: Which of the following correctly describes the futures pricing model used for each derivative type?

answer.text: Currency futures use Interest Rate Parity (IRP), equity futures use Cost of Carry, and bond futures use yield-based conversion factors
All three types use the same Cost of Carry model with identical inputs
Currency and equity futures use IRP while bond futures use the Black-Scholes model
Bond futures use Cost of Carry, currency futures use duration-based pricing, and equity futures use IRP

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Currency futures: F = S × (1 + R_domestic × t) / (1 + R_foreign × t) — Interest Rate Parity based on rate differentials. Equity futures: F = S × e^(r-q)t or S × (1 + (r-d) × t) — Cost of Carry minus dividend yield. Bond futures use conversion factors to relate traded bond to notional bond. **The Trap:** While all models are variants of no-arbitrage pricing, the specific inputs differ — IRP uses two interest rates, equity CoC uses risk-free rate minus dividends, bond futures involve delivery options and CTD bonds. **Cross-Exam Context:** This is a XIII-unique cross-asset comparison question that synthesizes pricing knowledge from Series I (IRP), IV (bond futures pricing), and VIII (equity CoC). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The pricing model always reflects the cost of replicating the futures position synthetically — different assets have different replication costs.
